your_wellness_goals.initial_health_goal_1: Reduce chronic lower back pain so I can sit through a full workday and drive without needing to constantly shift or stand up every 20–30 minutes.
your_wellness_goals.initial_health_goal_2: Improve overall flexibility and core strength so I can return to light jogging and weekend hikes without triggering a pain flare-up.
your_wellness_goals.initial_health_goal_3: Sleep through the night with minimal pain interruptions and wake up feeling rested enough to get through my workday without relying on afternoon caffeine.

how_are_you_doing.physical_changes:
Over the past two months, my lower back pain has gone from a constant 6–7/10 ache to more of a 2–3/10 most days, with only occasional spikes if I overdo it. I notice I can bend forward to tie my shoes without having to brace myself on the wall or on my knee, which used to be a big struggle in the mornings. My hamstrings and hips feel less tight, and I can now touch just past my shins when stretching, whereas before I could barely reach my knees.

I’m also able to stand and cook dinner for 30–40 minutes without needing to sit down, and I can carry grocery bags up the stairs to our apartment without that sharp pinching feeling I used to get in my lower back. Driving has gotten easier too—my commute is about 35 minutes each way, and I used to have to adjust my seat and shift around constantly. Now I can usually sit comfortably the whole drive with only minor stiffness when I get out of the car.

I’ve noticed that my posture has improved slightly; my partner has commented that I’m not slouching over my laptop quite as much. When I go for a walk, my stride feels more relaxed and less guarded, and there’s less tension in my lower back and glutes. Overall, I feel physically more stable and less fragile, like I’m not constantly about to “tweak” something.
how_are_you_doing.health_changes:
I’ve had fewer intense flare-ups that require me to take over-the-counter pain medication. Before starting care here, I was taking ibuprofen 3–4 times a week to get through tougher days at work. Now I might take it once a week, usually only after a long day of sitting in meetings or after I push myself too hard with yard work on the weekends.

I’ve also noticed fewer tension headaches, which I suspect were related to both my posture and stress levels. I used to get a headache at least once a week, often on Fridays, but over the last month I’ve only had one noticeable headache, and it was milder than usual. My digestion seems a bit more regular too, which might be related to drinking more water and moving around more.

I haven’t had any new illnesses, and when I did catch a mild cold about three weeks ago, it seemed to clear up faster than usual (about 3–4 days instead of lingering for a week or more). Overall, my body just feels a little more resilient and less worn down at the end of the day.
how_are_you_doing.emotional_changes:
Emotionally, I feel less on edge and less irritable, especially in the evenings when my pain used to be at its worst. Before treatment, I would come home from work exhausted and sore, and even small things—like my kids being loud or the dog needing to go out again—would make me snap more easily. Now that the pain is more manageable, I find I have a longer fuse and more patience.

I also feel less anxious about movement in general. I used to be constantly worried about “throwing out” my back again, especially after a bad episode last year when I had trouble getting out of bed for a couple of days. That fear made me avoid certain activities like playing on the floor with my kids or lifting heavier items. With the exercises and adjustments, I’ve started to trust my body more, and I’m less mentally preoccupied with the idea that any wrong move will set me back.

My mood overall has been more stable. I still have stressful days at work, but I don’t feel as overwhelmed or defeated by them. I’ve even noticed that I’m more willing to make plans with friends or say yes to family activities on the weekends because I’m not automatically assuming I’ll be too wiped out or in too much pain to enjoy them.
how_are_you_doing.energy_stress_levels:
My energy levels throughout the day have definitely improved. I used to hit a really hard slump around 2–3 PM, where I felt like I needed another cup of coffee just to get through meetings. These days, I still get a little tired in the afternoon, but it’s more of a normal dip rather than that completely drained feeling. I’ve been able to cut back from three cups of coffee a day to two, and some days I only have one in the morning.

I’m sleeping more consistently. I used to wake up 3–4 times a night because I couldn’t get comfortable or my back would start throbbing if I stayed in one position too long. Now I usually wake up once, maybe twice, and I can shift positions without that sharp jolt of pain. Because I’m sleeping better, I wake up feeling less groggy and more ready to start the day, which makes a big difference in my overall stress level.

In terms of stress, I feel like I’m coping better. I’ve incorporated some of the breathing and stretching routines you recommended, and I’ve started doing them during short breaks at work instead of just scrolling on my phone. Even five minutes of stretching at my desk seems to reset my body and calm me down. I still have a demanding job and family responsibilities, but I don’t feel as constantly “amped up” or reactive. Having a clearer plan for my health and seeing real progress has also reduced that background worry that I was just going to keep getting worse over time.
how_are_you_doing.new_health_challenges_or_stressors:
The main new stressor in my life has been increased workload at my job over the last month. Our team is short-staffed, and I’ve been taking on extra projects and staying later a couple of nights a week. This has made it harder to stick perfectly to my home exercise routine; there were a few weeks where I only did the exercises 3 times instead of the recommended 5–6 times. On those weeks, I did notice a bit more stiffness, especially in the mornings.

On the home front, my dad’s health has been declining (he lives about 45 minutes away), and I’ve been driving out to see him more often and helping coordinate some of his appointments. That’s been emotionally stressful and also means more time in the car, which used to be a big trigger for my back pain. Thankfully, the improvements I’ve had so far have made the extra driving more tolerable, but I can tell on those days that my back is a little more sensitive.

I haven’t had any brand-new injuries or major health problems, but I did notice a mild flare-up in my right hip after I tried to jog a full mile without building up slowly. It wasn’t severe, but it was a good reminder that I still need to pace myself and follow the progression you laid out instead of jumping ahead too quickly. Overall, the main challenges right now are time management, stress from work and family responsibilities, and remembering not to overdo it on days when I feel especially good.
